ISMA paper companion page

Understanding Oscillation
in Membrane-Based Mouthpiece Models

A compact physical model explains how a tunable membrane valve, nonlinear airflow and a modal resonator cooperate to transform steady blowing into self-sustained musical sound.

Musical acousticsMembrane valveInput impedanceHopf onsetTime-domain validation
Explore animationsDownload paper
Onset idea
silent → singing
The dominant eigenvalue crosses the imaginary axis when blowing pressure reaches the Hopf threshold.
1-DOFmembrane
Nmodes
Hopfonset

Physical model

The paper models the mouthpiece as a flow-controlled valve: the membrane displacement opens the channel, the resonator returns acoustic pressure, and the nonlinear flow closes the feedback loop.

Figure 1 from the article: membrane oscillator, active surfaces and resonator input.
Core equations
Membrane oscillator
ma ÿ + ca ẏ + ka y = S1 pb + S2 p
Modal input impedance
Zin(s)=Zc Σn=1N ( Cn/(s-sn) + Cn*/(s-sn*) )
Quasi-steady flow
u = 2π r2[y-y0]+ √(2|Δp|/ρ) sign(Δp) - Sa* ẏ
Hopf criterion
maxk Re{λk(J)} = 0,   fH = Im{λdom}/(2π)

Interactive animations

The animation translates the model into an intuitive loop: blowing pressure drives the membrane, membrane motion modulates the jet, and resonator feedback locks the oscillation frequency.

Pressure-to-sound transition

Move the blowing pressure slider

Below threshold, perturbations decay. Above threshold, oscillations grow and saturate as a finite-amplitude tone.

pb = 68%near onset
Resonator context

Input impedance selects the acoustic registers

The resonator response is represented by complex poles and residues. Peaks in |Zin| constrain the frequencies that can be amplified at onset.

f/fmnormalised frequency
|Zin|modal loading
3 peaksregister candidates

Results at a glance

Linear stability analysis maps the Hopf threshold over design parameters, while time-domain simulations confirm the near-threshold scenario.

Single-mode benchmark. The onset frequency remains close to the first resonator mode over most of the explored domain.
Time-domain validation. Below threshold the pressure decays; above threshold it grows toward a sustained oscillation.
Three-mode register selection. The active area ratio changes which register destabilizes first.

Paper narrative

From reduced physical model to onset maps: the page is designed as a compact companion to the ISMA manuscript.

Takeaways
1

A controllable exciter

A membrane mouthpiece offers tunable mass, damping, stiffness and pressure-exposed surface areas, making it a reproducible alternative to biological lips or reeds.

2

Dimensionless design variables

The non-dimensional form separates geometry, membrane tuning and resonator modal content, enabling clearer comparison across configurations.

3

Onset as a Hopf bifurcation

Linear stability predicts the blowing threshold and onset frequency; time-domain simulations verify the transition to sustained oscillations.

Selected references

Wilson & Beavers, “Operating modes of the clarinet,” JASA, 1974.

Hirschberg et al., “Musical aero-acoustics of the clarinet,” 1995.

Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments, 1998.

Chaigne & Kergomard, Acoustics of Musical Instruments, 2016.

Ablitzer, “Peak-picking identification technique for modal expansion,” Acta Acustica, 2021.

Velut et al., “How well can linear stability analysis predict...,” Acta Acustica united with Acustica, 2017.
